stories.title: The Widow-Curse of Blackpine
stories.text:
The widow Marta had worn her dead husband's shadow for forty days before she realized it was eating her from the inside—not with teeth, but with his unfinished rage. She'd stolen it from a Pelimor vault, paid a fence called Gareth in coin and a whispered oath. The shadow promised her vengeance against the merchant-lords who'd cheated Garen to his grave. Instead, it wore her like a coat, and every time she looked in still water, she saw his face layered beneath her own, mouth opening wider.

The shadow wanted to feed on guilt—not hers. It dragged her eastward, across the borderlands of the Cinderpeak Reaches, to a caravan encampment where a man named Kavic sat counting gold beneath a canvas pavilion. The shadow knew him. Had known him. Had died cursing his name.

Marta's hands moved without her will, drawing a knife. Kavic looked up, recognized something in her fractured eyes, and smiled—the kind of smile a man offers when he's been expecting a ghost.

"She sent you," he said. Not a question. "The one who brokered the theft. I've been waiting."

Marta's voice cracked like old leather. "The shadow—it wants—"

"Not the shadow. Me." Kavic rose, unbuttoning his sleeve to reveal a scar that writhed like a living thing. "I inherited Garen's debt when I inherited his gold. The shadow was just the delivery. Now we settle it properly—you, me, and the weight between us."

He drew a blade that caught the firelight wrong—it bent light like water—and Marta understood that some curses don't end in murder. They end in truth. The widow and the heir-to-guilt danced in the dust until dawn, neither winning, both bleeding, and when the sun rose, they walked into the Scorched Wastes together, the shadow finally silent at their heels.

stories.title: The Ledger of Ash and Ember
stories.text: In the waystation libraries of Pale Crossing, a young scribe named Aldwyn discovers that the refugee archive—Liv's counter-ledger of the Radiant Rising—has begun to *rewrite itself*. Each night, new entries appear in handwriting that isn't hers, describing events that haven't happened yet. The Auditor-General's agents are closing in on the hidden scriptoriums where Sevin distributes counterfeit aid. But Aldwyn realizes the phantom ledger isn't prophecy—it's a record of *choices not yet made*, and whoever controls the ledger controls which futures become real. When a refugee woman begs him to erase her name from the archive so her family will forget her sacrifice, Aldwyn must decide: is a false record that saves lives better than a true one that condemns them? The Radiant Rising's echo lies heavy on the borderlands, where water is scarce and the ledgers are the only currency that matters.

stories.title: Bone-Song at the Singing Scar
stories.text: Kess of the Drowned Hollows receives a summons—not in words, but in harmonic vibration that splits her bones like kindling. The Resonant Wound, a fracture in reality itself where the Sunken Cathedrals' echoes bleed into the mortal world, is widening. The transformed merchants are no longer singing in unison; their voices are *fracturing*, each one calling toward a different convergence point. Kess discovers that the pre-human civilization beneath the Tower isn't unified—it's *arguing with itself* across the centuries, and the seals are failing because the imprisoned entity has begun answering back. When she descends into the Wound to commune with the bone-language itself, Kess learns that the Radiant Rising didn't erase the old stars—it *renamed them*, and the old names are still trying to be spoken. A bone-singer from a rival tradition appears, claiming she can stop the Wound from widening, but her price is Kess's voice. The bone-resonance grows deafening.

stories.title: The Forger's Parliament
stories.text: Sevin the Auditor and Kalris the forger have been joined by a third figure: a mysterious woman calling herself the Archivist-Keeper, who claims to represent the "true Empire"—the one that existed before ledgers became more powerful than law. Together, they've established a clandestine parliament in hidden scriptoriums, creating counterfeit laws and trade licenses that function *better* than official ones. The scarcity from the Radiant Rising means their false rations are saving lives. But the Auditor-General discovers their operation and demands Sevin's surrender—in exchange, the Crown will legitimize the refugee archive as historical record. Kalris wants to accept; Sevin refuses. The Archivist-Keeper then reveals she was sent by someone much older, someone who remembers the pre-Rising world. She produces evidence that the Crown itself has been falsifying records for decades, and that every negotiated compromise will only perpetuate the lie. The forgers face an impossible choice: accept institutional legitimacy while knowing it's built on corruption, or burn it all and watch refugees suffer in the chaos. The revolutionary calculus demands a decision neither wants to make.

stories.title: The Transformed Merchant's Bargain
stories.text: Raen, a spice merchant half-transformed by the Radiant Rising, exists in the liminal space between human and living light. He can still speak, but only in the old language—the pre-human tongue—and his words reshape the air around him. A collector named Vex discovers that Raen is willing to *trade his remaining humanity* for something forbidden: the ability to perceive multiple timelines simultaneously. Vex realizes Raen isn't being consumed—he's *negotiating with his own transformation*, trying to understand what he's becoming. But the transformation-memories he seeks are being gathered by the Wanderer, who needs them to complete a ritual beneath the Obsidian Tower. When Vex refuses to broker the exchange, Raen chooses completion over safety and transforms fully into living light. Vex holds a single crystal of frozen amber containing starlight and watches him vanish into the sky. She cannot know if she saved him from annihilation or condemned him to an eternity of solitude in the spaces between worlds.

stories.title: The Deepkin Claim at Jade Coast
stories.text: Commander Vesta's harbor, built atop pre-human sacred ground, has become the focal point of a cold war. The deepkin are no longer negotiating—they're *claiming surface territory* in the name of the Sunken Cathedrals' harmonic resonance. Sylas the Deepwarden sends an emissary with a simple demand: evacuate the harbor, or the seals binding the abyssal rifts will be released deliberately. Vesta must broker an impossible treaty while refugees crowd the docks, waiting for ships that may never come. A young deepkin captain—Kaela, a Tidecaller Navigator—offers Vesta a secret: the deepkin leadership is fractured. Some want coexistence; others want the entire surface drowned. Kaela can swing the Council of Depths, but only if Vesta agrees to something unthinkable: sacrificing one-tenth of the harbor—and everyone in it—to the sea as a *gesture of good faith*. As the scarcity from the Radiant Rising deepens, Vesta watches the crowd on the docks and calculates how many she can save if she chooses to let ten thousand die.

stories.title: The Apprentice Sorcerer's Breaking Point
stories.text: The Apprentice Sorcerer—the young mage whose memory holds knowledge of nine iterations of reality—is beginning to *forget*. The seal she wears on her own consciousness, the one that keeps the forbidden knowledge locked away, is cracking under the pressure of the Radiant Rising's echoes. Fragments of other timelines are bleeding through: memories of worlds where the stars never reordered, where the pre-human civilization rose instead of fell, where the Wanderer never asked its questions. Multiple factions are hunting her—truth-seekers who want the knowledge, Auditors who want to suppress it, and something else: the *previous iteration of herself*, a version from a timeline that was rewritten, trying to warn her that unlocking the seal will cause a cascade collapse. When a faction-less scholar finds her in a waystation, offers her wine and kindness, and asks her to simply *speak what she remembers*, the Apprentice realizes the seal was never meant to imprison the knowledge—it was meant to imprison *her*. Saying the forbidden things might be her only way to become human again.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Blind Eyes
stories.text: Mardoc the Cartographer sacrificed his sight to create a deliberately incomplete map of the Obsidian Tower, one that blocks final convergence. But the maps he drew—while blind—have *continued to reshape the landscape*. Valleys appear where he drew them, rivers change course, mountains vanish. A trader named Hendren follows Mardoc's maps and discovers that the Cartographer is being hunted by something that moves through the blank spaces on his own charts: the Wanderer, or something wearing the Wanderer's questions. Hendren offers Mardoc a bargain—hide in the Shattered West, where geography itself is unstable and maps are useless. But Mardoc refuses. He's realized his blindness has given him the ability to *see* the Obsidian Tower's true shape: not a place, but a *process*, something unfolding across iterations and time. His incomplete maps aren't stopping the convergence—they're *delaying* it, asking it to unfold slower. And if he can stay blind long enough, stay lost long enough, perhaps the Tower will ask different questions. Hendren must decide whether to help a man who may be saving the world or hastening its doom.

stories.title: The Harbor-Master's Reckoning
stories.text: Captain Meren Blackwater, indebted to the sea via the Tear of the Drowned God, has been hearing the transformed merchants' true summons more clearly each day. The harmonic resonance from the Sunken Cathedrals is calling to him specifically, and he understands now what it's asking: that he *conduct his own fleet* down into the depths to witness the convergence. But Meren's crew depends on him, and the Empire has conscripted him to prevent others from making the pilgrimage. In a waystation tavern, Meren meets Sal, his first mate, and discovers that Sal has *already heard the call*—and is leaving with the tide. Sal doesn't ask Meren to come; he knows the Captain is bound by duty. But he leaves a chart, marked with the locations where the summons grows strongest. Meren stands at the crossroads: obey the Crown and stop the pilgrimage, or surrender to the sea-debt and lead his ship and crew toward transformation. The scarcity from the Radiant Rising means every ship matters; the fleet he commands could save thousands of refugees. But the sea is singing his name, and the debt always comes due.

stories.title: The Void-Echo's Last Question
stories.text: Vex the Merchant burned her trading post to sever her connection to the Void-Shards—fragments of the cosmic consciousness that Nethis sacrificed during the Radiant Rising. But the shards didn't disappear; they *accumulated* in the ashes and reformed as a single entity: the Shade-Echo, or something close to it. It appears to Vex not as predator or collector, but as a *mirror*—offering her forbidden sight: the ability to perceive what was fragmented during the Rising. The Shade-Echo claims it isn't hungry anymore; it's *grieving*, and it wants Vex to witness what was lost alongside it. Vex realizes the Void-Shards have been slowly reassembling themselves all along, and their reconstruction might trigger a *second Radiant Rising*—or birth something entirely new. When the Shade-Echo offers her one night in its company—not to consume her, but to *show her* the unmade cosmos—Vex must decide whether to bear witness to cosmic dissolution, or walk away from the only being that might understand what she has become.
